front: Architecture: four colossal seated statues flank the entrance, one with its entire upper body missing
Architecture: built by Ramesses II to commemorate his victory at the Battle of Kadesh
Architecture: inner sanctuary's back wall illuminated only twice a year, in February and October; Ptah's sculpture remains in the dark
Architecture: facade topped by a row of twenty-two baboons with their hands raised
Architecture: smaller of the two temples, dedicated to Queen Nefertari
Architecture: ceiling decorated with thousands of five-pointed stars against a deep blue background
Architecture: entire complex relocated by UNESCO in 1968 to avoid flooding from Lake Nasser
Architecture: relocation motivated the International Campaign to Save the Monuments of Nubia; led to the first UNESCO World Heritage Sites in 1972
Architecture: Madrid, Spain received the Temple of Debod as thanks for participation in preserving this site
Architecture: Italian circus strongman Giovanni Battista Belzoni cleared/blasted the sand from its entrance in 1817
Architecture: Champollion made his key breakthrough in deciphering hieroglyphs while reviewing texts from this site
Architecture: rediscovered by Jean-Louis Burckhardt in 1813 after being covered by sand
Architecture: Fifth Dynasty sources record expeditions to the nearby diorite quarries
